title: Universal interface fluctuations in absorbing-state phase transitions
authors: Yohsuke T. Fukai, Keiichi Tamai, Tetsuya Hiraiwa
abstract: Despite similarities between models exhibiting absorbing phase transitions (APTs) and those showing Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) growth, the relationship between these universal fluctuations has remained elusive. We numerically study (1+1)-dimensional interfaces of (2+1)-dimensional models showing APTs of directed percolation (DP) and compact directed percolation (CDP) classes with an active boundary, finding a universal crossover from short-time APT-governed fluctuations to long-time KPZ fluctuations. Upon rescaling time and length by the APT correlation time and length, the cumulants of the interface height distributions collapse onto a single scaling function. The fluctuation properties of the discrete Domany-Kinzel model and the continuum stochastic Fisher-Kolmogorov-Petrovsky-Piskunov (sFKPP) equation coincide, indicating that the KPZ growth parameters are determined solely by fundamental properties of the APT. For the CDP sFKPP equation, a dimensionless parameter tunes both the critical interface distribution and the KPZ parameters, with the interface properties of the biased voter model recovered in a limiting case. These results uncover a universal crossover in which KPZ fluctuations emerge from APT fluctuations at long times, linking paradigmatic universality classes of nonequilibrium scale-invariant phenomena.
summary: This paper demonstrates a universal crossover between two fundamental non-equilibrium universality classes: absorbing-state phase transitions and KPZ growth. It shows that at long time scales, the fluctuations of the active interface in APT models are governed by KPZ physics. This provides a fundamental link between the bulk dynamics of phase transitions and the scale-invariant growth of interfaces.
main_problem: The paper investigates the relationship between universal fluctuations in absorbing-state phase transitions (APTs) and Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) interface growth, specifically seeking to establish how bulk scale invariance relates to interface fluctuations.
main_result: The authors identify a universal crossover where short-time fluctuations governed by APT exponents transition into long-time fluctuations belonging to the KPZ universality class, with height distributions converging to the GOE-TW distribution.
method: Numerical simulations of (2+1)-dimensional discrete models and Langevin equations, combined with scaling analysis and parameter estimation using the Levenberg-Marquardt and Nelder-Mead algorithms.
model_or_system: The study utilizes discrete models such as Bond Percolation (DP class) and the Biased Voter Model (CDP class), alongside continuum stochastic Fisher-Kolmogorov-Petrovsky-Piskunov (sFKPP) Langevin equations.
key_observables: Interface height h(x, t), probability density functions of height, cumulants (mean, variance, skewness, kurt1), spanwise-averaged density, and spatial covariance.
important_parameters: Distance from criticality (epsilon), APT correlation time (xi_parallel) and length (xi_perp), and the dimensionless parameter D/sigma^2 for CDP models.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that height fluctuations follow the Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble Tracy-Widom (GOE-TW) distribution for KPZ fitting purposes.
figures_summary: Figures include schematics of interface height definitions, snapshots of active sites, plots of mean density and height probability distributions, and scaling collapses of cumulants and spatial covariance.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of APT-KPZ connection, describes the discrete and continuum models used, details the numerical simulation and parameter estimation methods, presents the results of the universal crossover and scaling collapse, and concludes with discussion on parameter universality and potential experimental candidates.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic and qualitative illustration of the interface growth process in the discrete models. (a) An illustration of the height definition for the discrete models with the active boundary. (b) Example snapshots of the active sites (black field) and the interface (red line) for the bond percolation (upper) and biased voter model (lower), respectively. The axis label and the scale bar indicate the length rescaled by the correlation length ξ⊥(ϵ) and the actual length, respectively. The rescaled time tξ = t/ξ∥(ϵ) is denoted in each plot. The values of ϵ are 1 × 10−5, 1 × 10−4 and 5 × 10−3 for the bond percolation and 1 × 10−6, 5 × 10−5 and 5 × 10−3 for the biased voter model,...

FIG. 3: The critical height distribution for the (left) DP-class and (right) CDP-class models. Data for the discrete models (black solid lines) and the Langevin equations [ρb = 0.1 for the left and ρb = 1 for the right] are shown. The dashed and solid curves show the data at t = 1 × 104 and t = 1 × 105, respectively.
